Christ, in his person, offices, and sufferings, is the main point of the gospel and should be the primary focus of a minister's preaching, but not so as to exclude other parts of God’s revealed truth and will. Paul preached the whole counsel of God. Few understand the fear and trembling that faithful ministers feel from a deep sense of their own weakness. They know how insufficient they are and are fearful for themselves. When only Christ crucified is plainly preached, the success must be entirely due to Divine power accompanying the word, and in this way people are brought to believe, to the salvation of their souls.

Those who receive the doctrine of Christ as Divine and, having been enlightened by the Holy Spirit, have examined it closely, see not only the plain history of Christ and him crucified but also the deep and admirable designs of Divine wisdom therein. It is a mystery made manifest to believers, though formerly hidden from the heathen world; it was shown only in dark types and distant prophecies, but now is revealed and made known by the Spirit of God. Jesus Christ is the Lord of glory—a title far too great for any creature. Many things people would not do if they knew the wisdom of God in the great work of redemption. There are things God has prepared for those who love him and wait for him, which our senses cannot discover, no teaching can convey to our ears, nor can it yet enter our hearts. We must take them as they stand in the Scriptures, as God has been pleased to reveal them to us.

God has revealed true wisdom to us by his Spirit. Here is proof of the Divine authority of the Holy Scriptures. As proof of the Divinity of the Holy Spirit, observe that he knows all things and searches all things, even the deep things of God. No one can know the things of God except his Holy Spirit, who is one with the Father and the Son, and who makes known Divine mysteries to his church. This is clear testimony both to the real Godhead and the distinct person of the Holy Spirit. The apostles were not guided by worldly principles; they had the revelation of these things from the Spirit of God, and the saving impression of them from the same Spirit. They declared these things in plain, simple language, taught by the Holy Spirit, totally different from the affected oratory or enticing words of man’s wisdom. The natural person, the wise person of the world, does not receive the things of the Spirit of God. The pride of carnal reasoning is as much opposed to spirituality as the basest sensuality. The sanctified mind discerns the real beauties of holiness, but the power of discerning and judging about common and natural things is not lost. But the carnal person is a stranger to the principles, pleasures, and workings of Divine life. Only the spiritual person is the one to whom God gives knowledge of his will. How little have people known of the mind of God by natural power! And the apostles were enabled by his Spirit to make it known. In the Holy Scriptures, the mind of Christ and the mind of God in Christ are fully made known to us. It is a great privilege for Christians that they have the mind of Christ revealed to them by his Spirit; they experience his sanctifying power in their hearts and bring forth good fruits in their lives.
